The work consists of localized demolition and removal of specific sections of the retaining wall located within the property limits of 770 Don Mills Rd, and not in the right-of-way (ROW), The sections of the wall are being removed due to the current alignment conflicting with the guideway Piers D-25 and D-26, to enable construction of these piers. Scope also includes demolition of the wall, implementation of dust control measures, and hauling/disposal of all demolition debris. Following demolition, the area will be gradedand prepared to meet the requirements for upcoming foundation and structural work associated with the guideway piers. This demolition is one component of the broader scope of work required to facilitate construction along the Ontario Line corridor.

Alternative Solution In accordance with Article 3.2.2.24. and Article 3.2.2.29., the building must be constructed of noncombustible construction. Article 3.2.2.16. Heavy Timber allows the use of heavy timber in a two storey sprinklered building in the following locations:Roof Assembly (Sentence 3.2.2.16.(1)),Structural members supporting the roof assembly in the storey below the roof (Sentence 3.2.2.16.(2)).Where a roof is intended to be used by an occupancy, Article 3.2.2.13 requires the roof to obtain the same rating as that of a floor assembly. In the case of the DMCRC, the roof requires the following ratings:No rating where the roof is not occupied (approximately 50% of roof), and6)Roof type R4,7)Specialty Furring Wall F-WDP1,8)Specialty Furring Wall F-WDP2,9)Ceiling type C-WD1,10)Exterior columns throughout.The intention is that the mass timber structure be exposed and therefore, not encapsulated in gypsum. Timber elements within exit stairs will be encapsulated to ensure that the flame spread rating of the interior finish is not more than 25, as per Table 3.1.13.2.All timber elements enclosing the exit stair will be encapsulated with two layers of 12.7 mm Type X gypsum.Several mitigating features are proposed to meet the Functional and Objective Statements of the OBC. The mitigating features will provide a performance level equal to or greater than that required by the acceptable solution in the OBC.The summary of mitigating features address the use of mass timber in a noncombustible building.The following mitigating features are provided:1)Sprinkler system increased reliability and density. The sprinkler system will be designed to an increased hazard level in accordance with NFPA 13, ¿Standard for the Installation of Sprinkler Systems¿. The entire building will be designed as an Ordinary Hazard, Group 1 (OH1) rather than a Light Hazard. Thus, the increase will result in 50% greater water delivered to the floor areas,2)Fire resistance rating and encapsulation. Exit stairs that are comprised of timber beams and columns will be encapsulated by two layers of 12.7 mm Type X gypsum. The floor assembly will provide the required 2 hour fire resistance rating where roof is occupied and is above the A3 occupancy.Sentence 3.1.1.6.(1) assigns heavy timber a fire resistance rating of 45 minutes for buildings permitted to be of combustible construction. In order to achieve a fire resistance rating with wood, the term mass timber is used. Mass timber can be assigned a greater fire resistance rating based on various ULC tests.Mass timber is comprised of large timber components such as glue-laminated timber (glulam), structural composite lumber (SCL) beams and columns, and cross-laminated timber (CLT). Mass timber elements have a significant amount of inherent fire resistance due to the large cross-sectional area and relatively slow rate of charring when exposed to combustion.Recently, the OBC has included encapsulated mass timber as a construction option. This requires the mass timber to be enclosed in gypsum or concrete to achieve the appropriate rating for buildings up to 12 storeys in building height.The DMCRC building will incorporate CLT elements that are not encapsulated in the following locations:Mass timber columns are located along the perimeter of the building and within the building. All columns are considered to provide a 2-hour fire-resistance rating, as per the architectural drawings. APPENDIX E contains the fire-resistance rating calculation methodology from CSA O86, ANNEX B.Second floor assembly will incorporate CLT, and concrete topping,Roof deck incorporates timber elements.The following items have been identified to have mass timber elements:1)Floor type FL3 and FL3a, Suspended CLT Floor with Concrete Topping,2)Exterior soffit S-1,3)Exterior soffit S-2,4)Roof type R1,5)Roof type R3, R3a, R3b,

The proposed alternative solution is for the elimination of tactile attention indicators around the pool deck of the public pool serving the mixed-use/residential building. OBC Clause 3.11.3.1.(14) requires a public pool be provided with a tactile attention indicator conforming to OBC article 3.8.3.18 between the pool deck and any area contiguous to the pool deck which may be confused with the deck. In consideration of the Functional Statements and Objectives associated with e OBC clause 3.11.3.1.(14), in lieu of providing tactile attention indicator, it is proposed to provide the following design characteristics to achieve sufficiency of compliance with the above OBC requirements: The 1800mm wide pool deck proper will be constructed of a different material than the remainder of the deck in the pool area, the raised wall of the pool will provide a physical boundary between the pool deck and the pool basin, and access into the pool area is fenced such that a person entering the pool area would have indication that a pool is near by.

Proposal to provide a transfer wall for the pool to achieve sufficiency of compliance with the requirement for barrier-free access to the pool. The proposed tranfer wall will be designed and installed in accordance with OBC sentences 3.12.3.2.(3) to (5). Related to 17 276108 BLD 00 NB.

The revised proposal is to develop a 12-storey (plus Mechanical Penthouse) residential building containing a gross floor area of 15,703.96 sq. m (169,036.02 sq. ft) and a density of 1.11 FSI. This provides for a proposed residential unit count of 285 dwellings, consisting of 80 studio units (28.07%), 113 one-bedroom units (39.65%), 77 two-bedroom units (27.02%), and 15 three-bedroom units (5.26%).There is approximately 570 sq m (6,135 sq ft) of indoor amenity space located on the B2 and ground floor, and 570 sq m (6,135 sq ft) of outdoor amenity space located on the ground floor, for a total amenity area of 1,140 sq m (12,270 sq ft) or a total 4 sq m per unit. Parking is proposed at-grade and contains a total of 50 parking spaces, including 16 visitor parking spaces and 9 accessible spaces. A total of 20 short-term bicycle parking spaces are proposed at-grade and 194 long- term bicycle parking spaces are proposed on the B2 level, for a total of 214 bicycle parking spaces.

This is the first application (Blocks 1A & 1B) for Site Plan Control related to the redevelopment of the former Celestica lands, now known as Crosstown.  This application proposes the construction of a nine storey office building having a daycare, retail uses at-grade, and a connection to the future Eglinton LRT Science Centre Station.  A public plaza, which will be subject to a POPS easement, is proposed west of the building.

The proposed plan consists of a 10-unit condominium townhouse development with associated underground parking. Six units are proposed to front Dundas Street West, while four units would front Chestnut Hills Crescent, with the underground parking structure being accessed off Chestnut Hills Crescent. The proposed development would have an overall gross floor area of 2,008 square metres and an Floor Space Index of 0.81.Rachel Godley and Adam Ward as co-counsel

Official plan amendment, zoning by-law amendment, and site plan application to finalize an LPAT settlement for a 10-storey and 32-storey residential buildings connected by a 2-storey podium with 551 residential units and an FSI of 2.29.  A new public road and a parkland dedication of 7,535.4 square metres is also being provided.

Revised submission implements modifications to the applications that were endorsed in principle by City Council during the spring and summer of 2014 in settlement of an outstanding Ontario Municipal Board (now Ontario Land Tribunal) appeals. The revised submission proposes two towers of 10 and 32-storeys connected via a 2-storey podium with a total of 551 residential units. A total of 672 parking spaces are proposed to be accommodated within a four-level underground parking garage.  Vehicular access to the site is proposed via the future Beecroft Road extension.  A 7,535.4 square metre public park is also proposed on the west side of the future public road.

The Proposed Building for 115 Dupont st. is a Group D 5 storey office building with a typical floor plate of 213 m2 and a gross floor area of 884 m2.The proposed building will contain 4 office units, one per floor, and includes 5 parking spaces and a lobby area at grade.

Proposed redevelopment of a former KFC restaurant site with a new 7-storey mixed use building containing 19 residential units (GFA of 2,199 m2) and commercial space on the ground floor (93 m2). A total of 18 parking spaces are proposed at-grade, of which 6 spaces will be accessed via parking.  stackers.   NOAC issued April 21, 2020 but Statement of Approval not yet achieved.. NOAC extended Feb 2, 2023 for two more years.   On October 8, 2024 applicant has come in with a revised proposal under the same application.  The contemplated development is 6 storeys and 26 units, and is consistent with the setbacks required by the Zoning by-law with an increase in setback to 13.48 metres from the south lot line abutting the existing residential property. The building floor plate has reduced to an average of 376.35 square meters and thus the overall gross floor area to 1996.34 square metres and a reduction in floor space index to 2.45. Additionally, the number of units has increased to 26 and with the introduction of studio apartments along with 1 bed, 2 bed and 3 bed typologies. The average size of units is 63.75square meters. The commercial area is located at grade with an area of 65.62 square metres and has been repositioned towards Danforth Road ¿ Byng Avenue intersection giving it additional frontage.  The onsite stacked parking is removed, thus reducing one parking space. The bike parking spaces have been increased to 33 long-term spaces in the basement and 7 short-term at grade.

A Development Application has been submitted to amend the Official Plan and Zoning By-law to allow for a long-term, mixed use plan for the entire Yorkdale Shopping Centre site. The original application included three conceptual block master plan options that comprised of a range of retail, office, hotel and residential uses with a maximum proposed total future gross floor area of 682,670 square metres and 2.29 Floor Space Index and an internal private above and below grade street network. The application has been revised into a single concept with expanded parkland and the inclusion of a public road. The proposal continues to propose a range of uses, including affordable housing which is intended to occur in a phased manner over the next 20+ years

The proposal is to construct a new 49-storey mixed-use building with three levels of underground parking. The ground level includes a shared residential and office lobby as well as retail and commercial uses fronting onto Danforth Avenue and a portion of Pape Avenue. Office uses are proposed from the third to fifth floors and the residential use is proposed from the sixth to 49th floors. Above the 49th floor is the mechanical penthouse and an indoor and outdoor amenity space. Additional indoor amenity spaces are located on the sixth and seventh floors and an additional outdoor amenity space is proposed on the sixth floor with access to the indoor amenity space. The proposal includes a total of 496 dwelling units, 29,756 sq. m (320,291 sq. ft.) of residential gross floor area (GFA) and 4,743 sq. m (51,053 sq. ft.) of non-residential GFA. The total proposed GFA is 9,997.4 sq. m, resulting in a FSI of 20.63.

The proposed development contemplates a 7-storey (25.1 metres) mixed-use building with a total of 2807 square metres of residential GFA and 125 square metres of commercial GFA at grade. 30 residential units are proposed, ranging in size from 1-bedroom units to 3-bedroom units. Separate entrances for the residential and commercial components are included in the front façade. Indoor amenity space is proposed on the 7th storey adjacent to outdoor amenity space.A road widening of 3.5 m is proposed along Danforth Avenue and a road widening of 1.17 m is proposed on the rear laneway. Access to parking is from the rear laneway. A total of 21 parking spaces are proposed, including 18 spaces for residents within a parking stacker system, and 3 spaces for visitors a grade, one of which will be an accessible space. 22 bicycle parking spaces are provided.

Site Plan Control application for Block 3A of 1245 Dupont Street that includes the construction of a 50 storey (156.95 metres) market rental building with 588 units and at grade retail. The building will have a total GFA of 41,239 square metres, of which, 408 square metres are for commercial uses. The proposal includes 100 parking spaces in two levels of underground parking. A total of 620 bicycle parking spaces are provided. Site is within an approved draft plan of subdivision. In accordance with the Section 37 agreement registered on title for this development, prior to first residential use or first condo registration on Block 3, the following are to be completed and/or conveyed to the City: - surface easements to the City over Private Strreet A (Record Lane), the Mews B Lands, and the POPs. - design for POPS and Mews B will be provided in next submission.

Above the 49th floor is the mechanical penthouse and an indoor and outdoor amenity space. The Proposed Development seeks to demolish the existing 7-storey building on-site to facilitate the construction of three towers at heights of 44, 47 and 49 storeys sitting atop a 5-storey podium (Tower A) and a connecting 6-storey podium (Tower B and C). Tower A will be 44-storeys and will sit atop a 5-storey podium. Tower B at 49-storeys, and Tower C at 47-storeys, will be connected by a 6-storey podium element that will form the streetwall along Deauville Lane. Rezoning application to redevelop the existing low-rise commercial building and associated surface parking area with a 33-storey (109 metres, plus mechanical space) mixed use building, with 445-residential units, and 29,009 square metres of residential gross floor area. The proposal comprises of a tower with a five storey podium that wraps the building on three sides. Surface parking and a two-level underground parking garage provide 149 vehicle parking spaces, including  eight spaces at grade. Note that Community Planning intends to bring forward this application to Scabrough Community Council on April 30th, 2026 for a decision report.
